content_text: Brainerd Hills, your new pavilion at Benham-Williams Park is officially open!  Huge kudos to everyone who worked to pull this off! This is the first "in-house" pavilion completely designed and constructed by our world-class team. Thank you to District 6 Councilwoman Jenni Berz and the Brainerd Hills Neighborhood Association for rallying the neighborhood for a rainy-day ribbon cutting and cookout in the park! It was a great chance to put this structure to the test, and it passed with flying colors. We are looking forward to opening more great structures like this in parks across our beautiful city.

Let's send another shout out to civil engineer and project manager, Elizabeth Goss, (holding paper) for letting us tag along and visit the equalization tanks near Brown Acres Golf course.  Both tanks are 240 feet in diameter and provide storage capacity of up to 15 million gallons each of excess wastewater during significant rain events.  This structure diverts excess wastewater, not flood water,  from existing buried sewer system pipeline when excess wastewater otherwise overflows into local waterways.   #CleanWaterMatters #waterquality #cleanwater #PublicWorks #engineers #civilengineering #womeninpublicworks #womenengineersrock

Join the City of Chattanooga Team Chattanooga , a vibrant and growing community of over 190,000 residents within a metropolitan area of nearly 600,000 is known as the "Scenic City" for a reason. Nestled along the Tennessee River and surrounded by the foothills of the Appalachian Mountains, we are a hub of outdoor adventure and innovation. Home to the University of Tennessee at Chattanooga (UTC) and Chattanooga State Community College , our city blends a rich industrial history with a tech-forward future—famously powered by the nation’s first community-wide "Gig" fiber-optic network.​ The City of Chattanooga was also recently designated as a "National Park City,” the first of its kind in the United States. Our Organization As a City, we are powered by a dedicated workforce of approximately ​ 2,600 employees committed to excellence in public service and making Chattanooga the best community in the country. Our team ensures our city thrives through a wide range of municipal services, including: Public Safety: Chattanooga Police and Fire Departments. Infrastructure Utilities: Public Works, Waste Resources, and specialized Stormwater management. Quality of Life: Award-winning Parks and Outdoors and Community Development systems and the Chattanooga Public Library. Transportation: The Chattanooga Metropolitan Airport (CHA) and citywide transit and pedestrian friendly initiatives. Innovation: Leading-edge smart-city programs with a commitment to cutting edge workforce development policies to ensure all our residents can get ahead. Why Choose Chattanooga? The City of Chattanooga’s workforce is the heartbeat of our community. We don't just offer jobs; we offer careers with purpose: making Chattanooga the best city in America. We are committed to being the premier employer of choice in Southeast Tennessee by providing: Competitive Compensation: Robust pay scales that reflect our commitment to our staff. Work-Life Balance: Generous paid time off and flexible scheduling where possible. Exceptional Wellness: Comprehensive health benefits and wellness programs designed for you and your family that deliver savings to you beyond your paycheck, featuring a centralized health clinic and pharmacy for all City employees. . Growth: A focus on professional development and upward mobility within the organization. ​The Great Outdoors: Your Backyard Playground: Chattanooga isn't just near nature, it's immersed in it. As one of the most biodiverse cities in North America, the "Scenic City" offers an ecological richness that few places on earth can claim. Whether you are an adrenaline junkie or a weekend wanderer, world-class adventure is always less than an hour away. ​Vertical Adventures: Scale legendary sandstone cliffs in what is widely considered the premier rock climbing hub of the Southeast. Water Rapids: From the high-octane whitewater kayaking of the Ocoee River to world-class bass fishing and peaceful paddling on the Tennessee River. Trails for Miles: Explore a massive network of mountain biking and hiking trails that wind through lush ridges, hidden waterfalls, and deep river gorges. In Chattanooga, "work-life balance" means you can finish your shift and be at a trailhead or on the water before the sun sets. Our mission is to work together to build a safe, vibrant, and sustainable city for all Chattanoogans. To do that, it takes a team of skilled and passionate individuals. So, if you’d like to call Chattanooga home – or maybe you already do! – and want to further your career through meaningful work that makes a true impact where you live, we’d love to talk to you. Salary : $64,024- $77,348 annually (GS.12) Work Hours: Monday - Friday, 8:00 A.M. - 4:30 P.M. (EST) *Hours may vary due to work assignments or projects.* Work Location: 100 E. 11th Street, Chattanooga, TN FLSA Status: This is an Exempt position. Department: Technology Services CLASSIFICATION SUMMARY: The Network Engineer Lead works under the general supervision of the Director of IT Infrastructure and is the Lead of the Network Engineering Division. The incumbent of this position will ensure the stable operation of the computer networks, including: planning, developing, installing, configuring, maintaining, supporting, and optimizing all network hardware, software, and communication links. This position will also analyze and resolve network related hardware and software problems in a timely and accurate fashion, and provide training where required to lower level support personnel. SERIES LEVEL: This is a stand alone position. ESSENTIAL FUNCTIONS: ( The following duties ARE NOT intended to serve as a comprehensive list of all duties performed by all employees in this classification, only a representative summary of the primary duties and responsibilities. Incumbent(s) may not be required to perform all duties listed and may be required to perform additional, position-specific duties.) Daily delegation of support tickets in a priority focused manner. Manage numerous concurrent tasks and assignments, with frequent interruptions. Managing a team including workload management, time off requests, performance evaluations and mentorship. Assist and mentor team members during the duration of an assignment or project when necessary. Serves as a subject matter expert for all networking. Ability to install software and hardware on networking equipment and microcomputers. Deploy LANs, WANs and wireless networks, including routers, switches, firewalls and other hardware. Ensure upgrades of all network hardware and related software is performed timely. Coordination with third party vendors for installations. Planning of new network build outs and refreshing of equipment and cabling. Monitor network performance and troubleshoot problem areas as needed. Maintain network performance history and provide network performance statistics and reports. Perform network asset management, including maintenance of network component inventory and related documentation and technical specifications information. Participate in managing all network security solutions. Perform all job responsibilities in alignment with the industry’s best security practices and regulatory guidelines to protect the confidentiality, integrity, and availability of protected information and other sensitive data. Assist with purchasing and acquisition of network components and services. Assist the Director of IT Infrastructure with short-term planning and long-term planning for network operations; works with end-users to understand utilization and networking needs. Assist with the development of procedures and standards pertaining to network operations. Receive, prepare and process a variety of reports, correspondence, inventories and other documentation as necessary to carry out job responsibilities and comply with departmental or city procedures. Participate in/on a variety of meetings, conferences, training sessions and/or other related groups in order to receive and convey information. Be available during the normal business hours, and may occasionally be asked to vary their schedule and/or work overtime to meet department requirements. Work will generally be performed using computer equipment in an indoor office environment. Some work will require outdoor configurations and installation. Setup or maintenance of some peripheral equipment may require work in areas such as data rooms or wiring closets with restricted room for movement. Must meet regular attendance requirements. Must be able to maintain good interpersonal relationships with staff, co-workers, managers and citizens. Must accomplish the essential functions of the job, with or without reasonable accommodations, in a timely manner. Performs other duties as assigned. DEPARTMENT SPECIFIC DUTIES (if any): MINIMUM QUALIFICATIONS: Bachelor's Degree in Information Technology, Information Systems, Computer Science or Similar and four (4) years of professional experience in data communications, networking, troubleshooting and repair, supplemented by the necessary years of education/technical training in data communications from a college or any combination of equivalent experience and education. LICENSING AND CERTIFICATIONS: Cisco Certified Network Professional (CCNP) Enterprise Cisco Certified Internetwork Expert (CCIE) Enterprise Infrastructure - desired. KNOWLEDGE AND SKILLS: Communicate professionally and maintain effective relationships with administrative and elected officials, vendors, outside government agencies, the public, and fellow employees through oral and written communication. Expert knowledge of network and routing protocols. Expert knowledge of switch configuration and programming. Expert knowledge of enterprise network designs. Expert knowledge of firewall administration (Zones, Policies, etc.) Checkpoint firewall experience is a plus. Continually gain new knowledge, and understand new technologies, by reading and understanding technical publications. Ability to determine, define and analyze problem definitions, requirements, and propose solutions. Ability to configure, test, and troubleshoot network equipment. Ability to work in a team-based environment. A complete understanding of OSI model. Ability to communicate effectively verbally and in writing. Ability to problem solve and think logically. A self-starter able to work independently or in a team environment. Ability to be a team player who is self-organized, self-disciplined, and eager to constantly improve themselves and team processes for efficiency and progress. Ability to adapt to a changing work environment. PHYSICAL DEMANDS: Positions in this class typically require reaching, fingering, grasping, talking, hearing, seeing and repetitive motions. WORK ENVIRONMENT: Sedentary Work: Exerting up to 10 pounds of force occasionally and/or a negligible amount of force frequently or constantly to lift, carry, push, pull or otherwise move objects, including the human body. Sedentary work involves sitting most of the time. Jobs are sedentary if walking and standing are required only occasionally and all other sedentary criteria are met. SPECIAL REQUIREMENTS: Safety Sensitive: N Department of Transportation - CDL: N Child Sensitive: N SUPPLEMENTAL INFORMATION: This position requires pre-employment screening(s) that include: background check / fingerprinting / CJIS check. Serves as a resource to city departments in developing, reporting, and utilizing metrics and data visualizations. Demonstrates the ability to present complex information related to data, performance and evaluation to diverse audiences and stakeholders. Utilizes the city's open government platform to create effective monitoring and reporting systems that tie into organizational decision ­making (e.g., budgetary, operational, strategic, and otherwise). Maintains and collaborates on designing the technology platform to allow for the effective monitoring of key performance indicators. Ensures the regular collection of data and subsequent reporting of data for the city within the organization and to external stakeholders. Develops and recommends operational strategies based on results of data analysis activities. Compiles data and statistics using quantitative and qualitative methodology. Employs principles and applications of mathematics and statistics, which may require knowledge of research methodology and techniques. Creates, updates, maintains and monitors the city’s automated Extract, Transform and Load (ETL) workflows connected to the city’s various applications and services. Shepherds open datasets through the open data workflow process prior to being posted on the open data portal. Works with representatives from city government and external organizations to coordinate contributions of data to a public platform or portal. Performs onsite data reviews to understand the business processes, data capture methods, in order to understand the data structure and value of the data being opened. Supports third party intermediaries wishing to access; and makes data accessible to different users through tools and applications. Master's Degree preferred. LICENSING AND CERTIFICATIONS: None KNOWLEDGE AND SKILLS: Ability to research and provide formal analyses and recommendations; Ability to analyze data to evaluate effectiveness and make recommendations based upon insights; Ability to understand both qualitative and quantitative aspects of data and performance measurement; Ability to work with web services API; Works collaboratively and maintains positive inter­departmental partnerships; Demonstrated capacity for innovation, self­-motivation, self-­learning and goal achievement; Strong critical and creative thinking skills to assess and resolve problems or issues by gathering and assessing information and using independent, professional judgment. Skilled in working with a variety of internal and external stakeholders; Skill in effective written and oral communications that articulate complex information and issues clearly and simply; Skilled in gaining insight from quantitative and qualitative data. Knowledge of Google Cloud data platforms and processes, like Big Query, Looker, Looker Studio, Google Workspace, or similar tools, with ability to learn new tools. Knowledge of various best practices for extracting, transforming and loading of data from one system to another system; Understanding of dataflow programming and transformations with ETL tools; Knowledge of GIS and mapping concepts (geocoding and projections), including common commercial platforms, as well as open source platforms; Knowledge of professional collection development principles; programming languages; creative problem solving; and open data concepts; Experience working with Python/R scripting for custom data transformation; Knowledge of Google Cloud data platforms and processes, like Big Query, Looker, Looker Studio, Google Workspace, or similar tools, with ability to learn new tools. A ribbon was cut today… but the real win happens underground. ✂️💧 The new Kanasita Equalization Basin and Pump Station Project can store up to 5 million gallons of excess wastewater during heavy rain events, helping reduce sanitary sewer overflows and protect our neighborhoods, waterways, and community. And that’s not all—this project also replaced aging infrastructure and addressed long-standing odor concerns for nearby residents. Not every superhero wears a cape. Sometimes it’s a 5-million-gallon tank quietly doing its job beneath the surface. 😉 Today, we celebrate another major milestone for Clear Chattanooga and a cleaner, more resilient future for our city. 📸 Drone photo courtesy of Cool New Media. #ClearChattanooga #Chattanooga #Infrastructure #CommunityFirst #WastewaterWorks

We hear your concerns about the odors, and we want to show you exactly what’s happening behind the scenes at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. Upgrading an aging wastewater system is a massive, highly complex process. In the video below, you can get a firsthand look at the major infrastructure upgrades we are currently undertaking to protect our waterways and improve our community: 💡Massive Infrastructure: We manage over 1,200 miles of underground piping and 80 pump stations to process 65 million gallons of wastewater from the surrounding region every single day. 💡Preventing Overflows: To protect the Tennessee River during heavy rains, we are utilizing 30 million gallons of new storage capacity. In fact, the clean water we put back into the river is actually cleaner than the river itself! 💡Odor Control: We know the smell is an issue we need to tackle. We currently utilize five different odor control systems to manage the greenhouse gases emissions from daily sewage, as well as the 80 different local industries that discharge into our system.  💡Green Energy Innovation: We recently announced a new initiative to convert wastewater methane directly into electricity. This will eventually take our campus off the electric grid and save up to $300,000 a month in energy costs. These upgrades take time, but our staff is working hard every day to move our city’s systems forward. Want to better understand the science behind it all? Tap the link below or in our bio for a simple breakdown of exactly how our wastewater treatment process works:👇 https://www.local3news.com/…/article_13c310a8-d534-479f-97c…

We hear you, Chattanooga! 🗣️ It’s no secret that facility upgrades have been a long time coming, and our team is hard at work making them a reality. We are proud to be working alongside Mayor Tim Kelly to implement these crucial improvements at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. Thank you to our community for your patience as we build a cleaner, fresher future for our city! Read more about the timeline and what’s underway below: 👇💧WHAT'S THAT SMELL? For years, Chattanooga residents have occasionally noticed unpleasant sewage odors drifting across parts of the city. Mayor Tim Kelly says significant improvements underway at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus should help reduce those smells in the coming years.⬇️

Wrapping up an incredible final day at the Tennessee Environmental Network Show of the South Conference! 🌿 A massive thank you to our speakers — Bill Cannon, Jacob McCrary, Travis Jones, and David Taylor — for representing the evolution happening within the Wastewater Department. We aren’t just a “sewer plant” anymore. We are stepping into our true identity as the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus. We’re shifting from stigma to stewardship, from waste to raw materials, and from consumption to circularity. That shift is exactly why we took home the 2025 Environmental Stewardship Award for Water Quality! 🏆 But we’re equally proud of what’s happening internally. From crushing 6,000+ work orders a year to launching our new employee-focused “Operation Career Ladder” this July, we are turning everyday labor into lifelong, empowered careers. Exciting things happening in Chatt 🔥🔥We’re here to answer the question you’ve been asking — what are those gigantic structures popping up along I-75? In a way to combat sanitary sewer overflows, our Wastewater Department has been hard at work with its partners to construct e2i2 Equalization Stations. During a storm event, these will: Send wastewater from the sewer lines to the diversion structure, that then funnels to a 48-inch gravity line (that’s about 20-ft deep) toward the pump storage. The pump storage will then send it to one of the two 15 million-gallon tanks (made of steel and shotcrete). This process will be powered by an electrical house with an 800kw generator and there will also be odor control ventilation for the pump station and tanks. Once the storm event is over, our Wastewater team will send the water downstream through the gravity line that connects to the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus where it will get treated before being sent back to the Tennessee River. Construction began in 2025 and is on track for its 2027 completion. So far, the project has also generated $39 million in local investment and supporting jobs in the region.

We are proud to introduce the new official logo for the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus! 🛡️ As our facilities and technologies have evolved, we wanted a logo that reflects the true scope of our work today. We are so much more than what goes down the drain; we are proud stewards of Chattanooga’s environment. Let us know what you think of the new look in the comments! 👇

Murray Hills Drive temporarily closed for sewer repairs Road expected to reopen March 27, weather permitting CHATTANOOGA, Tenn. (March 5, 2026) — Murray Hills Drive, located off Highway 58, is temporarily closed while crews with the City of Chattanooga Wastewater Department complete sewer repairs. The closure is necessary to repair underground sewer infrastructure and help prevent potential service disruptions. Contractors will perform excavations and point repairs to underground sewer lines through March 18. Once underground work is completed, crews will begin asphalt repairs and concrete restoration, which are expected to be finished by March 25. The roadway is expected to reopen March 27, 2026, after the concrete and asphalt have properly cured. Drivers should follow the approved detour route during the closure and allow additional travel time while work is underway. The construction schedule is weather dependent and subject to change. See the attached detour map below for the approved traffic route.

We're back on Facebook and excited to reconnect with our community! 👋 Did you know the Chattanooga Wastewater Department became an independent department in 2022? We are proud to serve you directly. One of our mottos? "We Take Your Crap Seriously" 💩 Sure, it gets a laugh—but behind the humor is a team working 24/7 to protect public health and the Tennessee River. (Fun fact: Our river system is widely considered the most biodiverse in North America—home to more fish species than all of Europe combined! 🐟). What does it actually take to keep Chattanooga flowing? Here is the breakdown: 🚛 COLLECT: We maintain 1,263 miles of sewer infrastructure (that’s the distance from Chattanooga to Boston!). 🧪 TREAT: We treat 230 million gallons per day at the Moccasin Bend Environmental Campus (enough to fill 350 Olympic pools). ✅ TRUTH: We don't just flush it. We treat every drop before it returns to the river. Save this post for reference! 📌 • Call 311: For sewer backups, leaky manholes, or billing questions. • Call (423) 643-7400: For sewer taps, location services, or new industry permits. Thanks for following along. We're here to serve!

Many assume a fresh coat of paint and replacement of old surface planks would suffice. In reality, the bridge requires extensive structural remediation. What does this entail? Essentially, it means that simply installing a new walkway on a deteriorating foundation is not a viable solution. This massive structure necessitates a thorough examination, and upon peeling away layers of repair, additional areas requiring attention are likely to be uncovered.

The final type of scam we're highlighting is law enforcement scams. In these cases, fraudsters will contact you while impersonating an officer, Federal agent, or bondsman. They'll claim you have an unpaid ticket, missed jury duty, have an unresolved warrant, or that you need to pay bond for a relative in jail, and then try to get you to pay them using gift cards, cryptocurrency, or digital payment services (Zelle, Apple Cash, PayPal, etc.). To be more believable, these scammers may even spoof a real law enforcement agency's phone number, tricking your caller ID. Here's what you need to know: 1. No legitimate law enforcement agency will ask you to pay fees, balances, or bonds with these payment methods. 1. No legitimate law enforcement agency will ask you to pay fees, balances, or bonds with these payment methods. 2. If you receive one of these calls, hang up immediately. 2. Today's Chattanooga Times Free Press includes a column, "Times Opinion: Chattanooga’s proposed budget has the wrong priorities."  While an opinion piece, facts and context matter. The following was sent to the paper and posted here to provide that information for all who wish to read about CPD's role in immigration enforcement...a topic CPD's Chief of Police has repeatedly publicly explained. Clarifying Mischaracterizations of CPD’s Role in Immigration Enforcement Thank you once again, Ben Sessoms, for providing the Chattanooga Police Department an opportunity to speak to our community about how its officers enforce laws regarding immigration and fill in the context that your column continues to omit. The section of Sessoms’ recent opinion column on the city’s budget that includes a section about immigration enforcement is factually misleading and reflects a misunderstanding of how local law enforcement operates. The assertion that CPD is engaged in “carrying out President Donald Trump’s ongoing mass deportation crackdown locally” mischaracterizes both federal and local roles and confuses the distinct systems of law enforcement that are in place in our city, state, and country.  This is especially important given Sessoms’ claim that “Of the over 500 immigrants transferred to federal immigration custody from the Hamilton County Jail in 2025, a majority of the initial arrests were made by the Chattanooga Police Department.” That statement requires context that his column does not provide. Transfers to federal immigration custody from the jail do not indicate that CPD is engaged in immigration enforcement or selective targeting based on immigration status. Rather, these transfers typically occur through standard criminal justice procedures following arrests for violations of state or local law.  It is important for community members, including local journalists, to understand that local police enforce state and local criminal laws, not federal civil immigration law. Immigration enforcement is the responsibility of federal authorities.  While Sessoms is certainly entitled and even paid to express an opinion, even opinion writers have an obligation to present complete and accurate facts to support their arguments. In this case, he could have easily referenced the broader coverage in the very paper that employs him for the additional context. Specifically, Siena Duncan’s reporting on CPD Chief John Chambers’ presentation to City Council earlier this year explaining in detail how the CPD is, in fact, not acting as an immigration enforcement agency but is operating within established law. Duncan’s reporting also notes that total arrests of Hispanic people by CPD declined 7% in 2025, and arrests of Hispanic people specifically for driving without a license declined 11%.  Again, thank you, Ben and Times Free Press, for the opportunity to present facts to your readers and our community. The most important takeaway from this is simple and straightforward: the Chattanooga Police Department does not make the law, officers enforce the law as written and do not target individuals based on where they come from, what they look like, or the language they speak. Elisa Myzal Director, Public Affairs Chattanooga Police Department

The next type of scam we're highlighting is phishing scams.  In these cases, scammers contact you by phone or email claiming to be from your bank or a company you have an account with (like Apple, Microsoft, etc.). Their goal is to get you to share personal or account information. They may try to trick you into giving these details by saying there's a suspicious charge and that they need you to verify your account number.  Unfortunately, these scammers are often successful. According to the 2025 FBI Internet Crime Report, financial losses from internet crimes are rising. In 2023, $http://12.5 billion in losses were reported, while 2025 saw $http://20.877 billion in reported losses.  If you think you've been the victim of a phishing scam, stop all communication immediately. Alert your bank as soon as possible to freeze your account, and file a police report with your local agency.

PSA: Fraud is on the rise!  Your best defense is knowledge, so over the next few weeks we'll highlight the top three types of scams and what to do if you think you've been the victim of one. First up, romance scams. There's nothing lovely about these scammers. They typically contact their targets on social media or dating sites, and can spend months or YEARS building trust before asking for money. Oh but they've sent you photos or videos? Those can easily be http://A.I. Please do not send money to a romantic partner you've never met in person. Think you've fallen for a romance scam? Here's what to do. 1. Stop all communication immediately 2. Alert your bank to report fraud and freeze your accounts 3. File a police report with your local department 4. Report cybercrime to the FBI at https://hubs.la/Q04kjSHD0 (https://hubs.la/Q04kkkkp0) 5. Report scams to the FTC at https://hubs.la/Q04kjPHK0 (https://hubs.la/Q04kjN6s0)

Ever wonder how our city parks do double duty as "natural air conditioners" and flood protection?  Our Natural Resources Manager, Anna Mathis, was featured on the National Recreation and Park Association Guardians of Greenspaces Podcast to talk about how we are using nature to tackle extreme summer heat. By "daylighting" streams, taking them out of underground concrete pipes and bringing them back to the surface, our team is creating living ecosystems right in the neighborhoods that need them most.  Tune in: https://shorturl.at/lwxIy #GuardiansOfGreenspaces”

Now that the dust has settled on the month of May, we want to take a second to look back at one of the busiest, most high-energy stretches our recreation facilities have ever seen. For four straight weeks, Chattanooga was the capital of Tennessee sports. We watched college softball powerhouses dominate at Frost Stadium, the state's top youth teams leave everything on the dirt at Warner Park, and elite high school athletes battle for state titles at the Champions Club tennis courts. So many great events for America 250! Hope to see you at all of them!Three posters. Three celebrations. One unforgettable summer in Chattanooga.  Introducing the official poster series created by local artist Chad Suggs for three of the City of Chattanooga’s America 250 Celebrations. Today we’re excited to reveal the official poster series for Chattanooga’s America 250 celebrations, created by local artist Chad Suggs. Each design highlights the creativity, culture, and community spirit behind these special events honoring America’s 250th anniversary. The America 250 Celebration Series includes: • June 28 — Democracy Speaks @ Renaissance Park A Poetry Pathways experience featuring poetry from 16 poets displayed on signage throughout Renaissance Park, alongside an evening of live poetry performances, spoken word, live music, and community gathering presented by Rhyme N Chatt and the City of Chattanooga Office of Arts, Culture & Creative Economy • July 2 — Pops on the River @ Coolidge Park A 26-year tradition continues as Chattanooga’s beloved summer celebration returns with live music, fireworks, food trucks, a Homegrown Vendor Market, Kids Zone activities, and performances by the Chattanooga Symphony & Opera • July 3 — Hamilton in the Round @ Renaissance Park A free outdoor sing-along screening of the hit musical Hamilton. Bring your lawn chairs and blankets for an evening of music, storytelling, and community under the stars. Special thanks to Chad Suggs for bringing this series to life and helping celebrate this historic milestone through art and design. This Tennessee America 250 Program is supported in part by the Tennessee Commission for the United States Semiquincentennial Commission.

Chattanooga is about to be booming with celebrations for America's 250th anniversary! From special programming put together by our Office of Arts, Culture and Creative Economy to the beloved Pops on the River — there's plenty of ways to honor over two centuries of patriotism and our history. We're even partnering with Hamilton County to send off Independence Day with the largest drone and pyrotechnics show in local history! Stay tuned as we roll our more details for these events.

Note, this is an ADDITIONAL Celebration for America 250 for Saturday night.  POPS on the River will be held Thursday, July 2nd at Coolidge Park, complete with music and firework show. Going to be an amazing week of celebrations!UPDATED AND CORRECTED: Hamilton County's celebration of America's 250th anniversary will include a drone show, two days after the usual fireworks event on the downtown riverfront. Looking for support to build stability and long-term success for your family? 💙 The Family Support Program is here to partner with you on your journey. Over six months, you’ll receive personalized coaching, skill-building, and connections to valuable community resources—all centered around your goals. What we offer: ✔ Budgeting & financial skill-building ✔ Resume & job-readiness support ✔ Goal setting & action planning ✔ Coaching and encouragement ✔ Referrals to additional resources You may qualify if you: • Live in Hamilton County • Are behind on essential household expenses • Have experienced financial hardship in the past 90 days • Are not currently in public or income-based housing 📍 Apply in person at: City Hall Midtown 6098 Debra Rd, Chattanooga, TN 📞 Call: (423) 643-6434 Take the next step toward stability and support—we’re here to walk alongside you every step of the way.

Some of our friends have been asking: “How do we get our events on the National Park City calendar?” 🌿 We love the energy! So we wanted to walk you through the steps to get your event live: How to submit: 1️⃣ Visit chattanooganationalparkcity.org 2️⃣ Head to the Events Calendar 3️⃣ Click “Join the Movement” to create your profile (it’s quick!) 4️⃣ Once you’re in, click “Create” and select “Event” to add your details. If your event is making Chattanooga greener, healthier, and wilder, we want to help you shout it from the rooftops! ⛰️ ✨ Bonus: We’ll be picking a few highlighted events from the calendar each week to feature right here on our social media! Let’s grow this movement together. See you outside! ☀️ #ChattanoogaNationalParkCity #GreenerHealthierWilder #ChattanoogaEvents #Nooga #NationalParkCity

The picnic may be over, but the growth is just beginning! 🌱✨ We gathered to celebrate our first batch of National Park City Seeds, and the energy was electric! ⚡ What started as place-based ideas for a better Chattanooga are officially shifting from “Seeds” to “Sprouts.” From World Fair Chattanooga to the Chatt Spots mental health bench on the Walnut Street Bridge, our community is proving that we don’t just dream — we grow. 🌿 We’d like to extend a massive thank you to @studioours.cha for hosting us in such a beautiful space, and to @theaugustsisters for the incredible art installation that brought the vision to life. You all provided the perfect environment for these ideas to take root. Now it’s time to follow along with your favorite “Sprouts” at npcseeds.org to see what “blooms” next! 🌸 #chattanooga #nationalparkcity #communitygrowth #npcseeds #cityinapark

👏👏👏👏👏Shoutout to Braxton Clayton for capturing some incredible photos of the new Wildwood Playscape at Enterprise South Nature Park—and for the craftsmanship behind it. Braxton’s carpentry work is all over this space, and it shows. The detail, creativity, and care that went into building Wildwood is something our community will enjoy for years to come. This project didn’t happen overnight. It’s the result of a dedicated crew putting in long hours, problem-solving, and bringing a shared vision to life. From raw materials to a one-of-a-kind nature playscape—this is what teamwork looks like. Grateful for Braxton and the entire crew who made this possible. Go take a walk, explore, and see their work firsthand. 🌿 #WildwoodPlayscape #enterprisesouthnaturepark #HamiltonCountyParks #OurParksOurPeople #BuiltWithPurpose #nationalparkservice

We are so close to bringing DuPont Park back to life, and the transformation is going to be incredible.  After lots of hard work from our staff and a generous grant from Norfolk Southern, the finish line is finally in sight, but the "finishing touches" are actually a pretty big undertaking. That’s why we are putting out a call for 50 volunteers to help us finish it up! If you’ve been looking for a way for your corporate team or civic group to get some dirt under your fingernails and do something that leaves a real, tangible mark on our community, this is the perfect moment to step up. We have a big day ahead of us, and we need every bit of that energy to get it done. We’ll be painting the pavilion, clearing back the overgrown vegetation, and laying down the lines for a new pickleball practice court and hopscotch areas for the kids. We’re even constructing a hammock pavilion that doubles as a climbing structure. It’s a lot of work, but it’s the kind of work that feels good when you see the results. Ready to sign up? Click the link below! https://www.ihelpchattanooga.org/need/detail/…

This canvas helps us map out the essential elements for success: Whether it’s a community garden, a local trail, or an urban greening project, we’re ready to plant. Over 200 birds spotter here! That's National Park City!Yesterday we went on a birding walk with @southeastconservationcorps at the Brainerd Levee. It was a bit windy, but we still got lucky! Some of the birds we saw incude American Pipit, Rusty Blackbirds, Red-Winged Blackbirds, Snipes, Osprey, Canadian Geese, Green-Winged Teal, Killdeer, Song Sparrows, Mallards, Red-Tailed Hawks, Barn Swallows, Grackels, Northern Rough-Winged Swallows, Black Vultures and more! Some of the photos of birds we got include: Mallards and Canadian Geese. Oh, and turtles. 🤷‍♀️🦆🐢  #outdoorchattanooga #conservation #cityinapark #outdoorchatt #riparianhabitat

Chattanooga is proud to be a National Park City, a place where people and nature live in harmony. But our work is never finished. We are launching a city-wide Seed Drive to gather the ideas and the energy needed to make our home even wilder, greener, and more connected. This series of events is an invitation for every Chattanoogan to help shape the future of our urban ecosystem. We aren't just looking for seeds; we’re looking for the next great idea to transform a vacant lot, support our pollinators, or bring more green to your block. Visit chattanooganationalparkcity.org for upcoming details, and follow us on Facebook and Instagram to see how you can help Chattanooga grow.
